chunks.text: Ptolemy II 10.0.1 Release Notes Ptolemy II 10.0.1 Release Notes Ptolemy II is a set of Java packages supporting heterogeneous, concurrent modeling and design. Its kernel package supports clustered hierarchical graphs, which are collections o

tered hierarchical graphs, which are collections of entities and relations between those entities. Its actor package extends the kernel so that entities have functionality and can communicate via the relations. Its domains extend the actor pack

the relations. Its domains extend the actor package by imposing models of computation on the interaction between entities. Examples of models of computation include discrete-event systems, dataflow, process networks, synchronous/reactive s

flow, process networks, synchronous/reactive systems, and communicating sequential processes. Ptolemy II includes a number of support packages, such as data , providing a type system, data encapsulation and an expression parser, plot ,

apsulation and an expression parser, plot , providing visual display of data, math , providing matrix and vector math and signal processing functions, and graph , providing graph-theoretic manipulations. The Ptolemy Book describes the Pto

manipulations. The Ptolemy Book describes the Ptolemy II design and the implementation of the Java classes. Ptolemy Book: Claudius Ptolemaeus, Editor, System Design, Modeling, and Simulation Using Ptolemy II, Ptolemy.org, 2014. ( Website , PDF

ing Ptolemy II, Ptolemy.org, 2014. ( Website , PDF (available via the installer, not via svn) ) Building Graphical Models describes how to use Vergil. Complete List of Domains in Ptolemy II Ptolemy II includes a growing suite of domains , each of whi

includes a growing suite of domains , each of which realizes a model of computation. It also includes a component library, in which most components are domain polymorphic , in that they can operate in several of the domains. Most

can operate in several of the domains. Most are also data polymorphic , in that they operate on several data types. The domains that have been implemented are listed below. Domains that are reasonably mature: Continuous : continuo

that are reasonably mature: Continuous : continuous-time modeling (redesign of the CT domain) DDF : dynamic dataflow DE : discrete-event modeling Modal : finite state machines and modal model (redesign of the FSM domain) PN : process networks wi

esign of the FSM domain) PN : process networks with asynchronous message passing Rendezvous : process networks with synchronous message passing SDF : synchronous dataflow SR : synchronous reactive Wireless : wireless Domains that are still exper

Wireless : wireless Domains that are still experimental: Algebraic : algebraic loops (New in 10.0) CA : cellular automata (New in 10.0) CI : component interaction (push/pull) CSP : communicating sequential processes DDE : distrib

communicating sequential processes DDE : distributed discrete events DT : discrete time Giotto : periodic time-driven GR : 3-D graphics HDF : heterochronous dataflow Petri Net : concurrent processes (New in 10.0) PSDF : parameterized s

nt processes (New in 10.0) PSDF : parameterized synchronous dataflow Ptera : ptolemy event relation actor (New in 10.0) Pthales : multidimensional dataflow (New in 10.0) Ptides : programming temporally integrated distributed

programming temporally integrated distributed embedded systems (New in 10.0) Ptinyos : interface to TinyOS TM : timed multitasking Platforms The core of Ptolemy II 10.0.1 is 100% Java, so it should work on any platform that has JDK

it should work on any platform that has JDK 1.6 or later. We developed Ptolemy II 10.0.1 under Mac OS X, Windows and Linux with JDK1.7.0_25 and JDK1.8.0_11 Under MacOS 10.7.5, we were also able to use Java 1.6.0_26. Ptolemy II 10.0 will

o able to use Java 1.6.0_26. Ptolemy II 10.0 will not compile under Java 1.3 because we use the java.lang.URI class, which is present only in Java 1.4 and later. JDK 1.5 or later is required so that these packages can use generics:

ed so that these packages can use generics: backtrack, ptalon and others. JDK 1.6 is required because ptdb uses javax.swing.GroupLayout and because of other changes. In the past, Ptolemy II has been compiled and run under IBM JDK 1

II has been compiled and run under IBM JDK 1.6.0. However, the IBM JDK has not be tried recently. There are the following limitations under IBM JDK 1.6.0 The Copernicus code generator does not work. Caltrop does not work. The IBM JDK see

s not work. Caltrop does not work. The IBM JDK seems to return directory contents in a different order than the Sun JDK. Thus actor.lib.io.DirectoryListing might return elements in a different order. Ptolemy II 10.0.1 Highlights Contents: High

order. Ptolemy II 10.0.1 Highlights Contents: Highlights Ontologies Export to web CG Code Generator FSM/Modal Model updates New demonstrations Building Controls Virtual Test Bed (BCVTB) Electric Power System (EPS) Fuel System Office Space Probabilist

System (EPS) Fuel System Office Space Probabilistic Models Ptango (Internet of Things) Other Key New Capabilities New and Enhanced Actor Libraries Additional features Works in Progress Bug fixes Highlights Ptolemy II 10.0 is the first complete relea

lights Ptolemy II 10.0 is the first complete release since Ptolemy II 8.0 and thus includes many changes. The key driving force for the release is to be a companion to the Ptolemy Book: Claudius Ptolemaeus, Editor, " System Design, Modeling, and Simu

maeus, Editor, " System Design, Modeling, and Simulation Using Ptolemy II ", Ptolemy.org, 2014. (included in the release as $PTII/doc/books/systems/PtolemyII_DigitalV1_02.pdf , but not present in the SVN developer tree.) Below are the highlights of t

SVN developer tree.) Below are the highlights of this release. Ontologies The Ptolemy II Ontologies package extends the Ptolemy II type inference engine to provide an efficient and scalable framework for general static analysis of arbitrary propertie

for general static analysis of arbitrary properties on a Ptolemy model. The framework supports properties that are specified as concepts in an ontology graph that must be structured as a lattice. The usefulness of this framework is demonstrated with

usefulness of this framework is demonstrated with an example of a physical dimensions ontology. This ontology labels different signals as representing acceleration, velocity, position, and time. In a Ptolemy model of physical dynamics, the dimensiona

Ptolemy model of physical dynamics, the dimensional properties of input and output signals can be automatically inferred using the ontology analysis. Ptolemy model developers can use the ontologies package to define their own ontologies for structura

ckage to define their own ontologies for structural and semantic property analysis. This can prevent interface connection problems during model construction by identifying when incompatible signals are erroneously connected. This work draws on concep

e erroneously connected. This work draws on concepts from compiler optimization static analysis techniques and applies them to Ptolemy actor-oriented models. References Patricia Derler, Elizabeth A. Latronico, Edward A. Lee, Man-Kit Leung, Ben Lickly

atronico, Edward A. Lee, Man-Kit Leung, Ben Lickly, Charles Shelton, " Ontologies ," a chapter from Claudius Ptolemaeus, Editor, " System Design, Modeling, and Simulation Using Ptolemy II ", Ptolemy.org, 2014. (included in the release as $PTII/doc/bo

rg, 2014. (included in the release as $PTII/doc/books/systems/PtolemyII_DigitalV1_02.pdf ) Ontologies Demonstrations CarTracking (Dimensional analysis) Abstract Interpretation Analysis Simple Constant/Nonconstant Analysis Simple Constant Analysis Exp

/Nonconstant Analysis Simple Constant Analysis Expression Monotonicity Analysis 2 Tank Model with Ontological Information Export to Web Ptolemy II includes a flexible mechanism for creating web pages from models and for building web services. The mor

from models and for building web services. The more basic mechanism is the export to web , which simply makes a model available as a web page for browsing using a web browser. Such a web page provides easy access and documentation for models that arc

easy access and documentation for models that archives both the structure of the models and the results of executing the models. It can be used to share information about models or their execution without requiring installation of any software, sinc

thout requiring installation of any software, since an ordinary web browser is sufficient. More interestingly, the mechanism is extensible and customizable, allowing for creation of fairly sophisticated web pages. You can associate hyperlinks or acti

ed web pages. You can associate hyperlinks or actions defined in JavaScript with icons in a model. The customization can be done for individual icons in a model or for sets of icons in a model. References Christopher Brooks, Edward A. Lee, Elizabeth

nces Christopher Brooks, Edward A. Lee, Elizabeth A. Latronico, Baobing Wang, and Roxana Gheorghui, " Web Interfaces ," a chapter from Claudius Ptolemaeus, Editor, " System Design, Modeling, and Simulation Using Ptolemy II ", Ptolemy.org, 2014. (incl

ation Using Ptolemy II ", Ptolemy.org, 2014. (included in the release as $PTII/doc/books/systems/PtolemyII_DigitalV1_02.pdf ) CG Code Generator Ptolemy II 10.0 includes the "cg" code generator at $PTII/ptolemy/cg . We took the lessons learned from $P

I/ptolemy/cg . We took the lessons learned from $PTII/ptolemy/codegen and applied them to cg. In particular, cg more easily supports multiple backends with less code duplication. The cg code generator is under active development, we are working on co

is under active development, we are working on code generation for large systems. For details about cg, see $PTII/ptolemy/cg/README.html . Developers: Christopher Brooks, Dai Bui, Bert Rodiers, Stavros Tripakis Resources $PTII/ptolemy/cg/README.html

os Tripakis Resources $PTII/ptolemy/cg/README.html Code Generation (CG) Demonstrations CompiledCompositeActor Clock and Ramp Time Delay Hello World! Maths Pi Square Calculus Pi Calculus Poisson Clock Discrete Event Code Generation ModularCG ModularCG

Discrete Event Code Generation ModularCG ModularCGPubSub Scale C Scale Java FSM/Modal Model updates References Thomas Huining Feng, Edward A. Lee, Xiaojun Liu, Christian Motika, Reinhard von Hanxleden, and Haiyang Zheng, " Finite State Machines ," an

, and Haiyang Zheng, " Finite State Machines ," and Thomas Huining Feng, Edward A. Lee, Xiaojun Liu, Stavros Tripakis, Haiyang Zheng, and Ye Zhou, " Modal Models ," chapters from Claudius Ptolemaeus, Editor, " System Design, Modeling, and Simulation

Editor, " System Design, Modeling, and Simulation Using Ptolemy II ", Ptolemy.org, 2014. (included in the release as $PTII/doc/books/systems/PtolemyII_DigitalV1_02.pdf ) ModalDemonstrations See Modal Model Demonstrations in the list of complete demon

Model Demonstrations in the list of complete demonstrations New demonstrations Building Controls Virtual Test Bed (BCVTB) The Building Controls Virtual Test Bed (BCVTB) website states: "The Building Controls Virtual Test Bed (BCVTB) is a software env

ontrols Virtual Test Bed (BCVTB) is a software environment that allows expert users to couple different simulation programs for co-simulation, and to couple simulation programs with actual hardware. For example, the BCVTB allows to simulate a buildin

or example, the BCVTB allows to simulate a building in EnergyPlus and the HVAC and control system in Modelica, while exchanging data between the software as they simulate. The BCVTB is based on the Ptolemy II software environment. The BCVTB allows ex

olemy II software environment. The BCVTB allows expert users of simulation to expand the capabilities of individual programs by linking them to other programs. Due to the different programs that may be involved in distributed simulation, familiarity

e involved in distributed simulation, familiarity with configuring programs is essential." The BCVTB demos use external tools, so they are not likely to run, though the models are browsable. C Room Matlab Room System Command Electric Power System (EP

tlab Room System Command Electric Power System (EPS) Overview ElectricPowerSystem - links to the models below: GeneratorTester ContactorTester GeneratorRegulator GeneratorRegulatorProtector GeneratorRegulatorFMUProtector GeneratorRegulatorProtectorSi

egulatorFMUProtector GeneratorRegulatorProtectorSimXRhapsodyFMU GeneratorRegulatorProtectorWithBus GeneratorRegulatorProtectorWithBusNotAsAspect GeneratorRegulatorProtectorTuLiP GeneratorRegulatorProtectorWithSpecificationAsAspect GeneratorRegulatorP

ectorWithSpecificationAsAspect GeneratorRegulatorProtectorWithDecorator GeneratorRegulatorProtectorWithBus GeneratorRegulatorProtectorWithProcessor GeneratorRegulatorProtectorPtides GeneratorRegulatorProtectorWithConstraintMonitor GeneratorRegulatorP

ProtectorWithConstraintMonitor GeneratorRegulatorProtectorWithSpecification Execute Demos ExecDemos RunDemos Fuel System 2 Tank Model Tank Model with Error Handling 2 Tank Model with Ontological Information 2 Tank Model with Bus communication Improve

mation 2 Tank Model with Bus communication Improved Tank Model Simple Tank Model Office Space UC Berkeley DOP Center Office Database Requires password to run UC Berkeley DOP Center Office Database Door Placards Requires password to run Probabilistic

r Placards Requires password to run Probabilistic Models Channel Fault Model Gaussian Mixture Model Gaussian Mixture Model Parameter Estimation Hidden Markov Model Analysis Hidden Markov Model Discrete-Time Markov Chain Ptango (Internet of Things) Pt

e-Time Markov Chain Ptango (Internet of Things) Ptango is a project between Bosch and UC Berkeley that involves the Internet of Things (IoT). The Internet of Things is the idea that everyday objects are uniquely addressable and have a represent

re uniquely addressable and have a representation in a structure similar to the Internet. Comparing and contrasting IoT with Cyber-physical Systems (CPS) is useful. The key thing with CPS is the interaction between the dynamic

h CPS is the interaction between the dynamics of the physical world and the dynamics of software and networks. The focus of IoT is on the information flow between physical devices and network services. In the Cyber-Physical Sy

d network services. In the Cyber-Physical Systems Concept Map, IoT overlaps with wireless sensing and actuating, though IoT is not necessarily wireless. The name "Ptango" refers to the fact that with the Internet of Things, it takes

t that with the Internet of Things, it takes more than two to tango. The research goal of Ptango is to invent a Model of Computation (MoC) for the Internet of Things that assigns operational semantics to the composition of Things. A

al semantics to the composition of Things. Applications and features of interest include: Building control/home automation Uniform device configurability Grouping/multicast/delegated control Data location transparency Audit trails Ptango is usi

a location transparency Audit trails Ptango is using Representational state transfer (RESTful) interfaces like http, which is stateless, where all the state is carried in the request and reply. Via RESTful interfaces, our work is fo

eply. Via RESTful interfaces, our work is focusing on a subset of IoT known as the Web of Things. BlinkyLight Chat Server Chat Client Chat Client2 Cookies DOPpresence Eplus70Actuator ErrorHandler Exception Guidance GuidanceSingleUserWithN

Handler Exception Guidance GuidanceSingleUserWithName OccupancyAlert OptimizationAdvisor SensorAndrew SmartRoom TemperatureSimulation UCBPower UCBPowerAnalyzerServer UCBPowerPtera WebServerDE Models of Computation Cellular Automata (CA) Conway's Game

e Code Generation (CG) Demonstrations above. Ontologies See Ontologies Demonstrations above. Type System Router Verification Simple Traffic Light (Requires NuSMV, see the Verification documentation) Linear Temporal Logic: Arbitor , Numerical , Priori

inear Temporal Logic: Arbitor , Numerical , Priority Arbitor Other Key New Capabilities Features Models for books located in $PTII/doc/books. We link to the book itself so that the models correspond with the book Claudius Ptolemaeus, Editor. " Syste

with the book Claudius Ptolemaeus, Editor. " System Design, Modeling, and Simulation using Ptolemy II," Ptolemy.org, 2014. Models for papers Small Code Tutorials $PTII/doc/tutorial/domains $PTII/doc/tutorial/graph $PTII/doc/tutorial/gui Build Infrast

utorial/graph $PTII/doc/tutorial/gui Build Infrastructure The build infrastructure now supports ant . Eclipse and ant are preferred over make because make is slower. To build using ant, see $PTII/doc/coding/ant.htm The tests can be run using junit fr

coding/ant.htm The tests can be run using junit from within ant. $PTII/pt-modules : Support for building OSGi modules of a subset of Ptolemy II: Hallvard Trætteberg The copyright file is now automatically generated from the licenses of the third part

ally generated from the licenses of the third party classes that are in the classpath. New and Enhanced Actor Libraries New Domain Polymorphic actors : ArrayUpdate Patricia Derler, Chris Shaver, and Edward Lee have developed a starting point for a mu

dward Lee have developed a starting point for a much smarter way of handling arrays in dataflow models. In particular, there is now in the Array library an ArrayUpdate actor, whose output is the same array as its input except that one element is

e array as its input except that one element is replaced with a new value. Please try it out for the radar model. The key here is that this is done efficiently for large arrays. The input array does not get copied. Instead, the output array r

does not get copied. Instead, the output array references the input array and encodes the diff. The input array is unchanged, and hence may be used safely by other actors to which it is destined without introducing nondeterminacy. This is onl

without introducing nondeterminacy. This is only a starting point because there are many other array operations that should be similarly encoded as diffs. There is a bit of work to be done here, and we need to make sure to be measuring perfo

, and we need to make sure to be measuring performance effects. The current implementation has the interesting property that makes copying arrays unnecessary most of the time. However, it has the unfortunate side effect of making copying arra

e unfortunate side effect of making copying arrays more expensive (we can no longer use the low-level System.arraycopy() mechanism in Java). It would be interesting to do a systematic performance evaluation of this. I believe that if we combi

e evaluation of this. I believe that if we combine this method with a PN director and a manycore machine, that we might get good utilization of parallelism on the machine. StringToBoolean , StringToDouble , StringToInt (Long Le) New Continuou

ringToDouble , StringToInt (Long Le) New Continuous actors : DCMotor Derivative PWM Waveform New Discrete Event (DE) actors : DateToEvent MostRecent New Syncronous Dataflow (SDF) actors : BootstrapSampleDelay New Synchronous (SR) actors : BinaryNonSt

pleDelay New Synchronous (SR) actors : BinaryNonStrictLogicGate Combine NonStrictAndGate NonStrictLogicGate NonStrictOrGate NonStrictXorGate Additional Features Aspect-Oriented Modeling $PTII/ptolemy/actor/lib/aspect AbstractInitializableAttribute Ac

actor/lib/aspect AbstractInitializableAttribute ActorExecutionAspect CommunicationAspectAttributes CommunicationAspect CommunicationAspectListener ConstantPublisherPort ExecutionAspectHelper ExecutionAspectListener ExecutionAttributes $PTII/ptolemy/d

AspectListener ExecutionAttributes $PTII/ptolemy/domains/de/lib/aspect HandSimDroid In 2011, the HandSimDroid project developed a prototype that allows Ptolemy models to run under Android. Students from CMU (Anar Huseynov, Justin Killian, Ishwinder S

om CMU (Anar Huseynov, Justin Killian, Ishwinder Singh) worked with Bosch researchers (Elizabeth Latronico, Charles Shelton) and Berkeley researchers to re-architect a portion of Ptolemy II so that models could run on a server and be displayed on a l

dels could run on a server and be displayed on a low-powered handset. The code for this effort is in $PTII/ptdb (Not compiled in the release because it uses Oracle GPL-like code) $PTII/ptserver (Not compiled in the release because it uses Oracle GPL-

ompiled in the release because it uses Oracle GPL-like code) $PTII/ptolemy/actor/injection $PTII/ptolemy/homer Classes were added to $PTII/ptolemy/actor/gui and $PTII/ptolemy/plot to support displaying on a remote machine. JavaScript The JavaScript a

g on a remote machine. JavaScript The JavaScript actor executes a script that can read inputs and parameters, perform calculates and write outputs. JavaScript JavaScript demos PDFAttribute PDFAttribute to the Utilities->Decorative library in Vergil t

e to the Utilities->Decorative library in Vergil that allows you to include PDF in a Vergil diagram. PDFAttribute can be used to include Latex-formatted equations in Vergil diagrams, and get full print resolution when printing to PDF for use in paper

t resolution when printing to PDF for use in papers, books, etc. PDFAttribute uses uses pdf-renderer, obtainable from https://pdf-renderer.dev.java.net/ . This is a LGPL'd open source product from Sun. To use PDFAttribute and create equations, we use

. To use PDFAttribute and create equations, we use LatexIt (see http://www.apple.com/downloads/macosx/math_science/latexit.html ) for the Mac version) From LatexIt, we can create a PDF file with an equation. Then drag into my Vergil diagram a PDFAttr

uation. Then drag into my Vergil diagram a PDFAttribute, or select Edit Custom Icon and drag in a PDFIcon. Double click on that to select the PDF file. Other new classes ptolemy.actor InstanceOpener IntermediateReceiver Librariable LocalClock Publish

n Mixture Model Parameter Estimation Hidden Markov Model Hidden Markov Model Analysis Discrete-Time Markov Chain Bug fixes For the current list of bugs, see Ptolemy II Bugs and Kepler Bugs . A few features and classes were removed outright. $PTII/pto

tures and classes were removed outright. $PTII/ptolemy/codegen Use $PTII/ptolemy/cg instead. Backward Compatibility Most models developed under Ptolemy II 1.0.1, 2.0.1, 3.0.2, 4.0.1, 5.0.2 or HyVisual 2.2-beta, 3.0, 4.0.1, 5.0.1, 6.0.2,

yVisual 2.2-beta, 3.0, 4.0.1, 5.0.1, 6.0.2, 7.0.1 and 8.0.1 should run under Ptolemy II 10.0.1 The MoMLParser includes a list of backward compatibility filters that make certain changes on models when read, handling such issue

ges on models when read, handling such issues as actors being moved or renamed and parameter names being changed. The filters themselves are defined in ptolemy.moml.filter . If you have developed your own actors under earlier

you have developed your own actors under earlier versions of Ptolemy II by writing your own Java files, you should recompile all your java code with the new release. In theory, copying the .class files should work, but recompiling

he .class files should work, but recompiling is safer. Features that were new in previous Ptolemy II releases Features that were new in previous Ptolemy II releases Limitations Ptolemy II 10.0.1 Limitations Page - Limitations found after releas

Limitations Page - Limitations found after release. Ptolemy II Bugzilla Version 10.0.1 has the following limitations: Release Limitations The gzipped tar file of Ptolemy II source files with Unix line endings ( ptII10.0.1.src.tar.gz ) requires GNU

ne endings ( ptII10.0.1.src.tar.gz ) requires GNU tar or WinZip. Solaris 2.5.1 tar will fail to untar this file with a checksum error because the pathnames are longer than 100 characters. Note further that some of the .class names are longer than

ther that some of the .class names are longer than 31 characters, which may cause problems when downloading zip files on the Macintosh and using Stuffit. The documentation for custom actors used in demonstrations is not included in the release

in demonstrations is not included in the release. The source code for the custom actors can be found in the source download. If a URL ends with #in_browser , then Ptolemy II will invoke the User's web browser. Unfortunately, this does not alwa

s web browser. Unfortunately, this does not always work, especially under Windows. The workaround is to exit Netscape and let Ptolemy II start up your browser for you. Web Start does not work very well if the pathname to the vergil.jnlp file has

well if the pathname to the vergil.jnlp file has spaces in it. The workaround is to place your Ptolemy II tree in a directory that does not have spaces in the path name if you plan on using Web Start. If your user library contains actors that are

art. If your user library contains actors that are not present in vergil, then you may see a dialog or dialogs asking if you want to skip these elements. This could happen if you had developed custom actors and put them into your user library and

om actors and put them into your user library and these custom actors are not present in the release. The work around is to click 'Skip Element' and continue with the start up. Web Start issues: The Matlab Wireless demo fails under Web Start.

he Matlab Wireless demo fails under Web Start. The problem is that the Matlab .m file cannot be found. The JAI Adaptive Median model does not work under Web Start - the repaired image might be corrupt. The reason is that it uses the JA

be corrupt. The reason is that it uses the JAI acceleration libraries, which Web Start does not load. Viewing a pdf file may bring up a blank window along with the pdf file. Running the code generator may bring up a blank window

e code generator may bring up a blank window while the java or javac processes are running. Some of the links on the GR domain instructions page might not work under Web Start because the files they refer to are not present. Under WebS

les they refer to are not present. Under WebStart, in the HOC ParallelExecution and SpontanousSources models, it is not possible to look inside the ThreadedComposites. In the ExportToWeb version of the HOC Case , the Documentation icon is

lemy configuration used to display models under WebStart does not support exporting to the Web. Limitations in the Class Mechanism There are several known problems: Several higher-order-components will not work inside class definitions. (M

onents will not work inside class definitions. (ModalModel, several Continuous actors, etc.). Name collisions can occur when adding objects to base classes whose names collide with objects in derived classes. Auto naming should look through the de

d classes. Auto naming should look through the derivedList for name conflicts. Propagation of changes to a base class is very slow in Vergil. If there are more than just a very few instances or derived classes, interactive editing is not practical

ved classes, interactive editing is not practical. As a workaround, put the class definitions in a separate file and edit them without opening the model that creates instances. The probable reason is that MoMLParser handles propagation by issuin

n is that MoMLParser handles propagation by issuing large numbers of change requests. Certain actors, such as MathFunction, change the number of ports when parameters change. This violates the derivation invariance for classes. To demonstrate th

vation invariance for classes. To demonstrate the bug, put a MathFunction actor in a base class that has a subclass. Change the function parameter in the subclass to anything but "modulo". Then change the function in the base class to "modulo"

change the function in the base class to "modulo" (which adds a port). Now attempt to insert a parameter into the port. You will get an InternalErrorException when it tries to propagate the change. Limitations in the Actor Libraries The actor li

e. Limitations in the Actor Libraries The actor library is far from complete. The Expression actor does not properly propagate type constraints in an expression-dependent fashion. It works correctly for double-valued expressions of double-valued in

for double-valued expressions of double-valued inputs. The Ptolemy parameter parser uses comma "," to separate elements in an array. This causes problems when Ptolemy is run on European machines that use a comma "1,0" instead of a period "1.1" i

hat use a comma "1,0" instead of a period "1.1" in floating point numbers. The Display actor uses the Swing JTextArea component which is known to have memory leaks. The readFile() parameter function does not work with applets. Instead, use readRe

n does not work with applets. Instead, use readResource(). IterateOverArray has the following problems IterateOverArray: When you drop in an actor, and then another actor, and then select "undo," the second actor is deleted without the fir

" the second actor is deleted without the first one being re-created. Thus, undo is only a partial undo. The fix to this is extremely complicated. Probably the only viable mechanism is to use UndoStackAttribut

mechanism is to use UndoStackAttribute.getUndoInfo() to get the undo stack and then to manipulate the contents of that stack directly. IterateOverArray: There should be an option to reset between firings of the inside ac

ion to reset between firings of the inside actor. IterateOverArray: If you drop a new actor onto an IterateOverArray in a subclass, it will simply acquire the new contents, but not actually do anything with them. It shoul

actually do anything with them. It should somehow refuse to accept the new object in the subclass, since to do so, it would have to delete the original object defined in the base class, and this would vio

in the base class, and this would violate the derived invariant. IterateOverArray: If an instance of IterateOverArray in a derived class has overridden values of parameters, those are lost if contained entity of t

hose are lost if contained entity of the instance in the base class is replaced and then an undo is requested. IterateOverArray: When an actor with a display icon like Const, Expression, or MathFunction is put into

Const, Expression, or MathFunction is put into IterateOverArray, the CopyCatIcon doesn't reflect the text display. Limitations in the PN Domain The PN domain documents that it locally handles mutations. However, this is current

handles mutations. However, this is currently not true in the implementation. For the basic PN model, this doesn't really matter, since mutations happen pretty much the same as they would otherwise. (i.e. they are non-determinist

would otherwise. (i.e. they are non-deterministic in when they execute) However, for timed PN models, there is actually some expressiveness lost, since timed PN models can locally execute mutations deterministically. Embedding a PN ty

ute mutations deterministically. Embedding a PN typed composite actor inside a non-process top level such as DE does not work. In fact embedding a process domain inside a non-process domain is likely to have problems. Profess Lee wrote:

in is likely to have problems. Profess Lee wrote: Yes, it looks as if the code is designed so that process domains (PN, CSP) can only be used within process domains. I'm not sure to what extent this is a limitation of the process domain

extent this is a limitation of the process domains vs. a semantic problem. What would PN mean within DE? Since PN has no well-defined notion of a "firing", how would you assign time stamps to the outputs of a PN actor? By defaul

stamps to the outputs of a PN actor? By default in DE, the time stamps of the outputs of an actor match those of the inputs that triggered the firing. There is no such notion in PN. Limitations in the User Interface Below are some of

mitations in the User Interface Below are some of the limitations of Vergil, the Ptolemy II Graphical User Interface. The labeling of ports gets messed up if you change cardinality. Cancelling a style change doesn't restore original state. Th

he tree view, or the top level window and the inside look of a composite component, when the top level window is closed, all other windows are closed, but the user is not prompted to save any change to the model. When setting the type of a port, w

e to the model. When setting the type of a port, we should provide a combo box that lists the common ports When creating a Modal Model, use Control-Right-Button to drawn arcs between nodes. One potential workaround would be to have an information

ential workaround would be to have an information message in the status bar about this. You should not change the names of built in ports - The port mechanism should disallow port name changes on ports that are named in Java. The UI could recogni

rts that are named in Java. The UI could recognize this. The UI should not allow the user to remove built in Java defined ports. Manually setting of the types of actors like ArrayToSequence that have constraints on their element types can cause

have constraints on their element types can cause problems. HTVQ demo is badly laid out when run under vergil. Weak support for multiports in the graph editor. It is difficult to control wire routing. Cannot have more than one vertex in a connection.

nd then run first using the run control panel. There is no mechanism in the UI to save a model as a class, nor to save a submodel as a class. There is no mechanism in the UI to create a custom icon for a library element and store it in the right p

for a library element and store it in the right place. If an actor uses a slider, then the actor tree navigator pane incorrectly shows the slider as a subitem of the actor. It seems that if you close a model that has been modified, get prompted to

e a model that has been modified, get prompted to save it, and select save, then the model is saved but not closed. It should be closed too... When I select a large number of items from one model, and copy and paste them into a new one, they some

and copy and paste them into a new one, they sometimes all get placed at the origin. Strangely, if I then auto-place, and then undo, they get their original (correct) placement! Printing a model that contains transparent objects can result in pr

hat contains transparent objects can result in problems when printing to Encapsulated Postscript (EPS). If a model has transparent objects, then when it is printed to EPS, the resulting file is a bitmap instead of a Postscript description of the g

tmap instead of a Postscript description of the graphics. You can tell by the file size (several Meg vs. several K) and by opening it in Illustrator, where in theory you should be able to ungroup and select and edit individual components. The wa

and select and edit individual components. The way Java prints EPS is that there is an implementation of Graphics2D that literally translates each graphics2D call into a Postscript snippet. If Java detects any transparent colors or images, then

a detects any transparent colors or images, then the literal translation of each element independently doesn't work, so the printing code reverts to writing everything to an image and then sending the image, encapsulated in Postscript to the print

the image, encapsulated in Postscript to the printer. Solution1: Use one of these pdf writing programs to generate pdf-1.4 compliant pdf files, which have extensions to support transparency that Postscript lacks, and then print them to eps, which

tscript lacks, and then print them to eps, which would use the distill implementation of the above algorithm. Or alternatively, we could switch to using pdftex instead of latex->dvips->distill which should embed pdf for figures directly at the e

ch should embed pdf for figures directly at the expense of less robust latex support. http://www.geocities.com/marcoschmidt.geo/java-libraries-pdf.html Solution 2: Use the undocumented flag -Dsun.java2d.print.pipeline=pdl on the command line to for

va2d.print.pipeline=pdl on the command line to force the printing to use the postscript path, ignoring transparency. Under Cygwin: export JAVAFLAGS=-Dsun.java2d.print.pipeline=pdl vergil Then edit the file by hand in Illustrator to add the trans
